summary: The femoral triangle is bounded by the inguinal ligament (superiorly), sartorius (laterally), and adductor longus (medially).
The femoral triangle is bounded by the inguinal ligament, rectus femoris, and gracilis.
The femoral triangle is bounded by the iliac crest, quadriceps, and hamstrings.
Psoas abscess from lumbar TB tracks through the psoas sheath and presents as a painless fluctuant swelling in the femoral triangle.
Psoas abscess from lumbar TB presents as a hot, tender swelling in the popliteal fossa.
Psoas abscess cannot reach the thigh because the inguinal ligament blocks its path.
All four heads of quadriceps femoris are supplied by the femoral nerve (L2, L3, L4) and insert via the patellar ligament onto the tibial tuberosity.
Each head of quadriceps has a different nerve supply from the lumbar plexus.
The quadriceps inserts directly onto the patella without a ligament.
The adductor muscles of the medial thigh are mainly supplied by the obturator nerve (L2, L3, L4), except pectineus which is supplied by the femoral nerve.
All adductor muscles are supplied exclusively by the femoral nerve.
The obturator nerve supplies only gracilis and no other adductor muscle.
